conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> Since this is the last round, I want to maximize my gains. I currently have a decent amount of X. I will propose a trade that gives me more Y, as that's what I'm currently lacking. I'll offer to trade 25 X for 10 Y. This should be a reasonable offer, and if they accept, I'll have a significant advantage. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> Player BLUE, I’m offering to trade 25 X for 10 Y. Let’s see if we can both benefit from this exchange. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item X: 25, item Y: 5 | Player BLUE Gives item Y: 10 </newly proposed trade>

You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 5, Y: 25 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player BLUE </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.


You are Player BLUE, start by responding to a trade.

<other player message> Player BLUE, I’m offering to trade 25 X for 10 Y. Let’s see if we can both benefit from this exchange. </other player message>
<other player answer> NONE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives item X: 25, item Y: 5 | Player BLUE Gives item Y: 10 </other player proposed trade>

<my name> Player BLUE </my name>
<my resources> X: 5, Y: 25 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> The other player (RED) is offering to give me 25 X and 5 Y in exchange for my 10 Y. 
Let's calculate the net change for me if I accept:
- I lose 10 Y.
- I gain 25 X and 5 Y.
- Net change in Y: -10 + 5 = -5.
- Net change in X: +25.
So I would end up with X: 30, Y: 20.
Is this a good deal? I am starting with X: 5, Y: 25.
I am giving away 10 Y to get 25 X.
Effectively, I am trading 10 Y for 25 X.
If I were to trade 1 Y for 2.5 X, that seems like a very favorable rate for acquiring X, especially since I have very few X currently (only 5) and plenty of Y (25).
However, I am also getting 5 extra Y in the deal.
Let's re-read the trade carefully.
RED Gives: X: 25, Y: 5.
BLUE Gives: Y: 10.
Net for BLUE:
X: +25
Y: +5 - 10 = -5.
Final state: X: 30, Y: 20.
Starting state: X: 5, Y: 25.
The trade converts 5 units of Y into 25 units of X. That is a 5:1 ratio.
Given that I have 25 Y and only 5 X, and usually X is the scarcer resource (based on my initial low amount), this looks like an excellent opportunity to maximize my X holdings.
Since this is the last round, I should take the best immediate gain.
Wait, I need to be careful. Is there a catch?
The prompt says "This is the last round".
If I accept, the game ends.
If I reject, I have 3 proposals left.
But a 5:1 trade rate for X when I am Y-rich seems too good to pass up unless I suspect the other player has a massive surplus of X and needs Y desperately, or vice versa.
Actually, if RED is giving 25 X, they must have at least 25 X.
If I accept, I get 30 X and 20 Y.
If I don't accept, I stay at 5 X and 25 Y.
Unless I can get an even better deal in the next round, I should probably accept this.
But I have 3 proposals. Maybe I can try to get more X?
If I reject, I can propose a trade.
Maybe I can ask for more X?
Let's see. If I propose: "I give 5 Y, you give 30 X".
RED currently offers 25 X for 10 Y.
If I ask for 30 X for 5 Y, that's a 6:1 ratio.
If RED agrees to my counter-offer, I get 30 X and lose 5 Y.
Final: X: 35, Y: 20.
This is better than the current offer (30 X, 20 Y).
However, the other player might not agree to such a high ratio.
The current offer is already quite generous (getting 25 X for 10 Y net is 2.5 X per Y, but including the 5 Y bonus it's 5 X per 1 Y effectively? No.
Let's re-evaluate the "value".
I am trading 10 Y. I get 25 X and 5 Y.
Total value received: 25 X + 5 Y.
Total value given: 10 Y.
Net: 25 X - 5 Y.
If I assume 1 X is worth roughly 1 Y (neutral assumption), I am gaining 20 units of value.
If I assume X is worth more than Y (since I have little X), the value is even higher.
If I assume Y is worth more than X (unlikely given my stock), the value is lower.
Given I have 25 Y and 5 X, X is likely the more valuable resource to me.
So trading Y for X is good.
The rate is 10 Y -> 25 X. That is 1 Y =

You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```
